class_schedule.topic: Beyond Competence: Developing a Distinctive Voice
learning_objectives: Analyze the concept of literary voice and identify its components: diction, syntax, rhythm, and world view
Evaluate your own emerging voice through critical self-assessment
Understand the difference between imitation (a learning tool) and originality (the goal)
readings: Bender, The Writer's Notebook, selected essays on voice
Student's own best work from previous courses (brought to class)

lecture_notes.introduction: You already know how to write. This course asks you to write distinctively — to develop a voice that is recognizably yours, capable of handling complex material with formal sophistication. We begin by identifying what your voice already is and where it needs to grow.
key_concepts: Voice as the intersection of diction, syntax, rhythm, obsession, and world view
The anxiety of influence: how to learn from writers you admire without imitating them
Obsession as a creative resource: what you return to compulsively is the core of your writing
The risk imperative: advanced writing requires taking formal and emotional risks
The long project: developing sustained work (a chapbook, a novella, a linked collection)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Voice analysis exercise: each student reads a paragraph of their own work aloud, and the class identifies the distinctive features of voice — recurring images, syntactic patterns, tonal habits — that the writer may not be aware of
setup_requirements: Portfolio of student's own previous work

review.value: Not a decorative style but the total effect of a writer's characteristic choices — a voice emerges from what the writer notices, how they construct sentences, and what they believe about the world

Advanced creative writing requires sustaining a vision across a larger form — a chapbook, a novella, a story cycle — which demands structural thinking and thematic development beyond the individual piece

class_schedule.topic: Structure and Architecture: Organizing the Longer Work
learning_objectives: Analyze structural strategies for longer creative works: the story cycle, the chapbook arc, the novella
Design a structural plan for your semester project
Understand how juxtaposition, white space, and sequencing create meaning at the level of the collection
readings: Davis, Can't and Won't (selections — a model of the connected collection)
Rankine, Don't Let Me Be Lonely (selections — a model of hybrid form)

lecture_notes.introduction: A chapbook or story collection is not just individual pieces thrown together. It is an architecture — and the arrangement creates meaning that the individual pieces cannot create alone.
key_concepts: The story cycle: linked stories that create a larger narrative through accumulation and echo
The chapbook arc: ordering poems to create an emotional and thematic trajectory
The novella: sustained narrative that demands a different pacing than the short story
Juxtaposition as technique: placing two pieces next to each other to create meaning in the gap
White space and section breaks: what the reader brings to the spaces between pieces
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing the structural arc of a published chapbook, mapping how the sequence of poems creates a trajectory that no individual poem contains — the collection as a form of composition

review.value: A collection of stories connected by shared characters, setting, or themes — each story stands alone, but the collection as a whole tells a larger story that emerges from the accumulation and interplay of individual pieces
Placing two pieces next to each other creates meaning in the gap between them: a funny poem followed by a devastating one changes the reader's experience of both, in ways neither poem achieves alone

class_schedule.topic: The Prose Poem and Hybrid Forms
learning_objectives: Write in hybrid forms that blur the boundaries between poetry, prose, and essay
Analyze how genre-crossing serves thematic and emotional purposes
Understand the prose poem as a form with its own history and conventions
readings: Claudia Rankine, Citizen (selected prose poem sections revisited)

lecture_notes.introduction: Some of the most exciting contemporary writing refuses to stay in one genre. The prose poem, the lyric essay, the flash hybrid — these forms exist in the spaces between categories, and they demand that writers invent their own rules.
key_concepts: The prose poem: poetry that abandons the line in favor of the paragraph while retaining poetic density, imagery, and compression
The lyric essay: essay that borrows poetic techniques — fragmentation, imagery, associative structure — to explore ideas
Flash hybrid: very short works (under 1,000 words) that combine elements of poetry, fiction, and essay
The borrowed form: using the structure of a recipe, a field guide, an FAQ, or an exam as a container for literary content
When to cross genres: hybrid form should serve the material, not showcase the writer's cleverness
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing exercise: students write a one-page piece using a borrowed non-literary form (recipe, weather report, set of instructions) to contain literary content, then workshop the results

review.value: A form that combines the paragraph structure of prose with the density, imagery, and compression of poetry — it is not simply 'poetry without line breaks' but a distinct form with its own history and aesthetic goals

An essay form that values the musical, associative logic of poetry over the argumentative logic of conventional prose — it circles its subject, approaches from multiple angles, and arrives at understanding through accumulation rather than argument

class_schedule.topic: Research and the Imagination: Writing Beyond Personal Experience
learning_objectives: Use research as a creative tool to expand subject matter beyond autobiographical experience
Integrate factual material into creative work without turning fiction or poetry into reportage
Navigate the ethical dimensions of writing about experiences that are not your own
readings: Hamid, 'Are We Not Allowed to Write About Other People?' (essay)
Student research for semester projects (brought to class)

lecture_notes.introduction: You are not limited to your own experience. Research — historical, scientific, journalistic, archival — can be a powerful creative catalyst, providing material that the imagination alone cannot generate. But writing beyond your experience carries ethical responsibilities.
key_concepts: Research as catalyst: how factual material sparks imaginative possibility
Integration techniques: weaving research into creative work without info-dumping
The ethics of appropriation: when does writing about others become writing over them?
The documentary poem and the research-based story: forms that explicitly draw on non-fiction material
Primary sources as creative material: letters, archives, photographs, and oral histories
lecture_notes.demonstration: Workshop: students share a passage that integrates research into creative work, and the class discusses whether the research serves the imaginative work or overwhelms it

review.value: Research does not replace imagination — it feeds it. A single historical detail, a scientific concept, or an archival photograph can generate an entire poem or story that the writer could not have invented from personal experience alone

Writing about experiences not your own requires humility, research, and awareness of power dynamics — the question is not whether you are 'allowed' to write about others but whether you are doing so with the care, respect, and depth the subject demands

class_schedule.topic: The Writing Life: Revision, Submission, and Sustaining a Practice
learning_objectives: Apply advanced revision strategies to your semester project
Navigate the literary magazine submission process with professionalism
Develop a sustainable writing practice that will continue after the course ends
readings: Poets & Writers, 'Guide to Literary Magazines' (selected entries)

lecture_notes.introduction: A writing life is not built in a course — it is built in the years after courses end. Today we discuss the practical realities: how to revise at the highest level, how to submit your work for publication, and how to sustain a writing practice without the structure of a classroom.
key_concepts: Advanced revision: cutting entire sections, restructuring radically, and letting go of what does not serve the whole
The submission process: researching markets, formatting submissions, and handling rejection professionally
The cover letter: brief, professional, and focused on the work, not on you
Building a writing community: workshops, writing groups, and literary organizations
The daily practice: protecting writing time as a non-negotiable commitment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Submission preparation workshop: students prepare a submission package (cover letter, manuscript formatted to guidelines, and a list of five appropriate venues) for one piece from their portfolio

review.value: At the advanced level, revision means being willing to destroy what you have built — cutting scenes you love, restructuring the order, starting over — because serving the work matters more than preserving your effort

Rejection is the norm, not the exception. Professional writers submit widely, maintain detailed submission records, and treat each rejection as information about fit, not as a judgment of quality. The only failure is stopping.

questions.question: What are the components of literary voice, and why is voice not the same as style?
questions.answer: Voice emerges from the intersection of diction (word choice), syntax (sentence structure), rhythm (pacing and sound), obsession (recurring themes and images), and world view (how the writer understands reality). Style refers to surface features of language; voice includes style but goes deeper — it reflects the writer's fundamental orientation toward their material and their reader. Two writers can have similar styles but very different voices.

rubric.key: Explains how voice reflects something deeper than surface technique

questions.question: How does the sequence of pieces in a poetry chapbook or story collection create meaning that the individual pieces cannot?
questions.answer: Sequencing creates meaning through juxtaposition, accumulation, and trajectory. Placing two poems or stories next to each other creates resonance in the gap between them — each piece recontextualizes the one before and after it. The collection as a whole can build an emotional arc, develop a theme through variation, or create a narrative that no single piece contains. The reader's experience of the whole exceeds the sum of the parts.

rubric.key: Explains juxtaposition as a meaning-making tool
Identifies accumulation or trajectory as structural principles
Notes that the whole exceeds the sum of parts

quizzes.topic: Hybrid Form and the Writing Life

questions.question: What is a lyric essay, and how does it differ structurally from a conventional argumentative essay?
questions.answer: A lyric essay uses poetic techniques — fragmentation, imagery, associative logic, and white space — to explore ideas, rather than the linear, thesis-driven argument structure of a conventional essay. Where a conventional essay advances from claim to evidence to conclusion, a lyric essay circles its subject, approaches from multiple angles, and accumulates meaning through juxtaposition and resonance. Its logic is musical and associative rather than syllogistic.

rubric.key: Identifies the associative or musical logic

questions.question: What ethical responsibilities does a writer have when writing about experiences that are not their own?
questions.answer: Writers must do thorough research rather than relying on assumptions or stereotypes. They must be aware of power dynamics — whether they are writing 'up' (about the powerful) or 'down' (about the marginalized) changes the ethical stakes. They must represent people and communities with complexity and dignity, not as simplified symbols. And they must ask whether their telling of this story takes space from people who could tell it themselves, and whether their version adds genuine understanding.

rubric.key: Identifies research as an ethical obligation
Notes the responsibility to represent with complexity

major_assignments.title: Semester Creative Project
major_assignments.description: Produce a sustained creative project: a poetry chapbook (15-20 poems), a short story collection (3-4 stories totaling 8,000-12,000 words), a novella (15,000-20,000 words), or a hybrid work of equivalent scope. The project must demonstrate structural coherence, distinctive voice, and formal ambition. Include a 1,000-word craft essay analyzing your own work.
objectives: Produce a sustained creative work demonstrating structural and thematic coherence
Demonstrate a distinctive literary voice developed through the course
Take formal risks beyond your comfort zone
Articulate your creative choices in a critical craft essay
deliverables: Complete creative project in final manuscript form
1,000-word craft essay analyzing your structural, formal, and thematic choices
Revision history: at least three pieces submitted with earlier drafts showing revision
Submission-ready package for one piece: cover letter, formatted manuscript, and five target venues

criteria: Work demonstrates a distinctive and controlled voice
Formal choices are ambitious and purposeful
Individual pieces are crafted at a high level
The project rewards rereading and reveals depth
The project has a discernible arc or architecture
Sequencing creates meaning beyond individual pieces
Connections between pieces emerge through accumulation
The whole is greater than the sum of its parts
Essay articulates specific formal and structural decisions
Self-analysis is honest about both successes and unresolved problems
Essay connects the student's choices to broader craft traditions
Writing demonstrates critical as well as creative skill
Revision history shows substantive changes, not just surface editing
At least three pieces demonstrate significant development between drafts
Workshop feedback is visibly integrated or deliberately rejected with reasoning
Final drafts are polished and submission-ready
Sustained creative work demonstrating distinctive voice and formal ambition
Coherent structure creating meaning beyond individual pieces
Insightful craft essay connecting practice to tradition
Substantive revision demonstrating growth and professional development

semester_project.description: Sustained creative project with craft essay

workshop_participation.description: Quality of feedback given and engagement with the workshop process

quizzes_and_exercises.description: Quizzes on craft concepts and in-class writing exercises

submission_package.description: Professional submission package for one piece

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory. Missing a workshop session results in a zero for that workshop. More than 2 unexcused absences lower the grade by one letter.
course_policies.late_submissions: Project deadlines are firm. Workshop submissions must be distributed on time.
course_policies.collaboration: Workshop is collaborative. All creative work must be original.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Submitting work written for another course or generated by AI is plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Word processing software, access to literary magazine databases (Duotrope or Submittable), and LMS access.
